steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Two independent clauses are connected by the conjunctive adverb "on the contrary." Left side: "One might assume cases like this...to be [rare]" — a complete sentence. Right side: "they are counted among a litany of examples of Stigler's law" — a complete sentence. Rule: conjunctive adverbs require a semicolon before them and a comma after them: [IC]; on the contrary, [IC].

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
A. rare on the contrary, — no punctuation before "on the contrary" and no semicolon separating the two independent clauses. Creates a run-on. Reconstruct: "...to be rare on the contrary, they are counted..." — the two sentences blur together. Eliminated.

B. rare, on the contrary; — the semicolon is placed after "on the contrary" rather than before it. The semicolon must come before the conjunctive adverb, not after. Reconstruct: "...to be rare, on the contrary; they are counted..." — misplaced. Eliminated.

C. rare, on the contrary, — a comma before "on the contrary" is a comma splice; it cannot join two independent clauses. Reconstruct: "...to be rare, on the contrary, they are counted..." — two full sentences joined by a comma. Eliminated.

D. rare; on the contrary, — semicolon before the conjunctive adverb, comma after. Reconstruct: "...to be rare; on the contrary, they are counted among..." — follows the rule exactly. Correct.
